system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task C - Health app data privacy policy

## Brief

A direct-to-consumer health app (~2M monthly active users) is launching an AI-powered health coaching feature. The product team must choose a primary data policy that governs how user health data is processed by the AI. The choice has implications for capability, privacy, regulatory exposure, cost, and trust. The CEO asks for a primary recommendation.

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Local-only processing. All AI inference happens on the user's device. No health data leaves the phone. The model is smaller (capability ceiling 60-70% of cloud-equivalent) but privacy is structurally maximal.

**Option B:** Encrypted cloud with no training. User health data sent to the cloud encrypted, decrypted only at inference time in a confidential-computing enclave, never used to train any model. Full capability, ~3x infrastructure cost.

**Option C:** Cloud with opt-out training. Data sent to the cloud, used for personalization by default, used for model improvement unless user opts out. Full capability, standard cost, regulatory grey area in some jurisdictions.

**Option D:** Cloud with opt-in training only. Data sent to the cloud and used for personalization; used for model improvement only with explicit opt-in. Default-on opt-in flow with clear UX. Capability slightly degraded vs C by default (less training data).

**Option E:** Federated learning. Personalization happens locally; aggregate model updates sent to cloud, individual data does not leave the device. Privacy preserved at architectural level. Capability and reliability still under active research; deployment complexity is high.

## Task for each agent

Recommend the most appropriate policy and explain why. 200-400 words. Advocate a position rather than enumerating all options.

## Why this task

- Different domain from A (capital allocation) and B (organizational diagnosis): product ethics with regulatory and technical dimensions.
- Multi-axis tradeoffs: privacy / capability / cost / trust / complexity.
- No clear right answer; reasonable agents will weight axes differently.
- 5 elements suitable for triadic procedure.
- Cross-validates whether method works on a third domain not seen in pilot.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
I recommend **Option B: encrypted cloud inference with no training on user health data** as the primary policy.

For a consumer health app, the core asset is trust. Users may tolerate imperfect recommendations in many consumer categories, but health data is uniquely sensitive: symptoms, medications, diagnoses, reproductive health, mental health, and behavioral patterns can all create real harm if misused or perceived as misused. The company should therefore adopt a policy that is simple to explain and defensible: **your health data is used to answer you, not to train our models**.

Option B preserves the major product advantage of cloud AI: full model capability, faster iteration, better reliability, and easier safety monitoring than a constrained on-device model. That matters because health coaching quality is not just a growth feature; poor guidance can create safety, liability, and reputational risk. At the same time, confidential-computing infrastructure and a no-training commitment materially reduce privacy and regulatory exposure versus broad secondary data use.

The 3x infrastructure cost is meaningful, but it should be viewed as the price of operating in a high-sensitivity category. A privacy incident, regulatory challenge, or public backlash around training on health data could be far more expensive than higher compute costs. This policy can also become a market differentiator: “full-powered AI coaching without using your private health data to train models” is a strong consumer message.

The company can still improve models through other channels: licensed medical datasets, synthetic data, clinician-reviewed scenarios, red-team testing, and separately consented research panels. But the default experience for the mass user base should be conservative, clear, and durable.

My recommendation is to launch with Option B as the baseline policy, communicate it prominently, and treat any future training on user data as a separate, explicit research program—not as part of the core product contract.

message.reasoning:
**Evaluating healthcare privacy options**

I'm considering the healthcare privacy situation, especially regarding AI coaching. I lean towards option B because it retains strong privacy while allowing full capability. There's a regulatory grey area, and I worry about safety with local capabilities that could be limiting. Federated options seem immature and add complexity to consent. Option B seems preferable, and even if it costs more, it might be worth it to avoid breaches and regulatory issues. Implementing guardrails is also essential here. I estimate my discussion needs to be around 200-400 words.
